title: How to Navigate Psychedelic Experiences When You Are a Control Person
body:
How to Move through Psychedelic Experiences When You Are a Control Person

Look, if you’re someone who likes to keep a tight grip on things - your schedule, your emotions, your plans - then stepping into a psychedelic experience can feel like stepping into a hurricane. It’s wild, unpredictable, and downright unsettling. Right?! You want to know what’s coming next. You want to steer the ship, but suddenly, the ocean’s in charge. And that’s scary.

Here’s the thing, though: being a control person doesn’t mean you’re doomed to a rough trip. It just means you have to approach it differently. Psychedelics don’t care about your spreadsheets or your perfectly color-coded calendar. They operate on their own terms. But you can set yourself up to ride the wave instead of getting wiped out by it.

Why Control People Feel So Uncomfortable on Psychedelics

First, let’s get clear about what being a “control person” means. You might be that person who plans every detail of a vacation down to the minute. Or maybe you’re the one who feels anxious if you don’t know exactly what’s going to happen in a meeting or how a conversation might end. You like predictability, order, and a sense of mastery over your environment. You probably even take pride in being the "planner" or the "fixer" in your group.

Now drop that into a psychedelic experience. What happens? The usual rules don’t apply. Time warps, thoughts spiral, emotions surface out of nowhere. There’s no parking lot to leave your car in, no user manual. Your mind feels like it’s on a rollercoaster that you didn’t buy a ticket for. It’s a vulnerability that’s hard to sit with.

Does that land? You’re not alone. Many who’ve tried have felt this way, myself included. But here’s a thought: what if your control impulse could be gently softened, just enough to let the experience unfold without feeling like you’re drowning?

Before You Start: Prepare Like a Pro

Preparation is your best friend. Seriously. It’s where you can use your natural skills to create a container that feels safe. Some ideas:

  • Set a clear intention. Write it down. What do you want from this experience? Healing? Curiosity? Emotional release? Keep it simple and concrete.
  • Choose your environment wisely. This is your “safe zone.” Soft lighting, comfy pillows, maybe a soft therapy blanket (paid link) to wrap yourself in. Familiar music playlist ready. No surprises.
  • Limit your dose. Start small. The unknown is intimidating enough without throwing you into a deep dive. Microdosing or a light dose helps you learn the ropes.
  • Have a sitter or guide. Someone you trust, who understands your control style and won’t push you. Their job is to be your anchor, not your boss.

These are concrete steps you can control ahead of time, so you can soften your grip when the experience starts. You’re still the captain, just letting the wind take you for a bit.

During the Experience: Letting Go Without Losing Yourself

Once you’re in it, your brain will try to grab control anyway. You’ll catch yourself thinking, “Okay, what’s next? Can I get us back to normal?” That’s your control wiring kicking in. It’s natural. Here’s how to work with it, not against it.

  • Use your breath. A simple tool but powerful. Slow, deep breaths. Count to four on the inhale, hold for four, exhale for four. Repeat. It’s a way to anchor attention when the mind races.
  • Name what’s happening. If you’re feeling anxious, say it out loud or in your head: “I’m feeling anxious.” Labeling emotions can lessen their grip. Know what I mean?
  • Focus on the senses. Feel the blanket’s texture, listen to the music, look at a plant nearby. Grounding yourself in the present moment reduces overwhelm.
  • Remember the intention. When your mind flares up, bring it back. “I’m here for healing.” “I’m here to explore.” It’s a thread you can follow when things feel chaotic.

Honestly, it’s okay if you can’t let go completely. Control isn’t going to vanish just because you took a psychedelic. It’s more of a dance. You lean in, then step back. You hold on, then release. That ebb and flow is part of the process.

After the Experience: Integration Is Where Control Can Shine

Here’s where your love of order can actually serve you big time. Integration means making sense of what happened, putting it into words, turning it into something useful.

You might want to:

  • Journal. Write down everything you remember. Don’t worry about making it perfect. Just get it out. What images stood out? What feelings bubbled up? Did any insights come through?
  • Talk it through. Whether with a friend, a therapist, or a group who gets it, sharing your story helps solidify the experience.
  • Set small goals. If you learned something about yourself, what’s one tiny step you can take to bring that lesson into your daily life?

It’s the part where control actually feels good - organizing your thoughts, creating a plan, making progress. And if you want a guidebook to the science and stories behind these experiences, check out How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan (paid link). It’s a great read that blends facts with personal narrative, which can help you feel more grounded in the unknown.

Real Talk: When Control Feels Like a Trap

Control can be a double-edged sword. It protects you, but it can also box you in. Sometimes, you’ll find yourself resisting the experience so hard that it makes things worse. Panic, confusion, or even a bad trip can follow.

Here’s an example. I once worked with a woman named Sarah, who was a classic control person. She planned her psychedelic trip down to the minute, but when the experience shifted unexpectedly, she freaked out. She tried to force her mind back to where she wanted it, spiraling into anxiety.

What helped her was a simple phrase I gave her: “This moment is just a moment.” She repeated it like a mantra. It didn’t solve everything, but it gave her permission to stop fighting. She started breathing, noticing sensations, and eventually the storm passed.

This kind of practice - acceptance, presence - isn’t about giving up control forever. It’s about choosing when to hold on and when to let the current carry you. Does that make sense?

Tools You Can Use Right Now

If you want to build a toolkit that respects your control style but gently expands it, try these:

  • Guided meditation apps. They structure your attention for you. Even 10 minutes a day helps build tolerance for uncertainty.
  • Books that explore control and letting go. “Stealing Fire (paid link)” by Steven Kotler and Jamie Wheal is a fascinating dive into states of flow and altered consciousness. It’s science-based but accessible, perfect for curious control people.
  • Comfort items. A cozy blanket, like a soft therapy blanket (paid link), or a favorite sweater can ground you physically when your mind is spinning.

And if you want to read more about how to prepare and recover from psychedelic experiences, check my article Preparing for Psychedelics: A Guide for Beginners.

Science and Safety: What the Research Says

Truth is, psychedelics are powerful tools but not magic bullets. Research from places like Scientific American shows that mindset and setting massively influence outcomes. If you’re used to being in control, psychedelics will challenge that, but with proper preparation and support, many find it deeply healing.

And remember, these substances are not for everyone. If you have a history of psychosis or certain mental health conditions, they could be risky. Talk to a healthcare professional before exploring.

Finding Your Own Way

Look, you don’t have to throw your control out the window to have a meaningful psychedelic experience. You just need to find your balance. Use your strengths - your planning, your attention to detail, your ability to create a safe space - and combine them with a willingness to sit with uncertainty for a bit.

It’s a process. Sometimes messy. Sometimes beautiful. It might feel like a tug of war between your old habits and new possibilities. But over time, you can learn to trust both yourself and the experience.

If you want to keep exploring this path with me, head over to kalesh.love. I share tools and stories that help control people, and everyone else, find their way in the psychedelic world with less fear and more curiosity.

And if you want to hear from someone who’s been through it and written about it with raw honesty, check out A Really Good Day by Ayelet Waldman (paid link). Her story about using psychedelics to manage mood swings is relatable and real, not sugarcoated.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
